symptoms_text: Angular water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown to black. Spots are bounded by leaf veins giving angular appearance. Leaves may dry and fall off. Black lesions on stems and bolls. In severe cases, blackarm symptoms on stems with dark streaks.
symptoms_visual: Dark brown to black angular spots on leaves between veins. Water-soaked appearance initially, turning dry and papery. Black elongated lesions on leaf petioles and stems.
favorable_conditions: High humidity above 80%, temperature 25-35°C, wind-driven rain, close plant spacing
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like Suraj, LRA-5166. Remove and destroy infected plant debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Maintain proper spacing. Delint seeds with sulphuric acid.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Streptocycline 0.01% for 30 minutes. Spray Streptocycline 100 ppm + Copper oxychloride 0.25% at disease onset. Repeat spray at 15-day intervals.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens at 10g/kg seed. Apply Trichoderma viride in soil.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Practice 3-year crop rotation. Avoid fields with previous cotton bacterial blight history.
source: CICR Nagpur / AAU Anand Package of Practices

causal_organism: Cotton leaf curl virus (transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci)
symptoms_text: Upward or downward curling of leaves. Thickening and darkening of leaf veins. Small leaf-like outgrowths (enations) on underside of leaves along veins. Stunted plant growth. Reduced boll formation.
symptoms_visual: Leaves curled upward or downward into cup shape. Veins appear thickened and dark green. Small leaf-like projections visible on leaf undersurface. Plants appear bushy with shortened internodes.
favorable_conditions: High whitefly population, temperature 25-30°C, dry weather favoring whitefly, late sowing
cultural_management: Sow resistant/tolerant varieties. Early sowing (April-May). Remove infected plants immediately. Avoid ratoon cotton. Use yellow sticky traps for whitefly monitoring.
chemical_management: Control whitefly vector: Spray Imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.5ml/L or Thiamethoxam 25 WG at 0.5g/L. Alternate with Spiromesifen 22.9% SC at 0.75ml/L. No direct cure for virus.
biological_management: Release Encarsia formosa parasitoid for whitefly control. Neem oil spray 5ml/L as repellent. Install yellow sticky traps at 12/acre.
prevention: Early sowing before May 15. Destroy previous crop residue. Avoid planting near other host crops. Monitor whitefly with yellow traps from 30 days after sowing.

symptoms_text: Yellowing of leaves starting from lower leaves progressing upward. Wilting of plant despite adequate soil moisture. Brown discoloration of vascular tissue visible when stem is cut lengthwise. One-sided wilting common. Plant death in severe cases.
symptoms_visual: Lower leaves turn yellow then brown and wilt. Plant droops during hot part of day, may recover at night initially. Cross-section of stem shows dark brown ring in vascular tissue. Asymmetric yellowing of canopy.
favorable_conditions: Soil temperature 25-30°C, acidic to neutral soil pH, sandy soils, presence of root-knot nematodes, continuous cotton cropping
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties. Practice 4-5 year crop rotation with non-host crops like sorghum, wheat. Apply lime to raise soil pH above 7. Good drainage. Remove and destroy infected plants.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Carbendazim 50% WP at 2g/kg seed. Soil drenching with Carbendazim 1g/L around infected plants. Apply Trichoderma enriched FYM at 2.5 kg/hectare.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride at 4g/kg seed. Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens at 2.5kg/ha in soil. Use Trichoderma enriched compost.
prevention: Avoid continuous cotton cultivation. Manage root-knot nematodes. Use wilt-resistant varieties. Deep summer plowing to expose soil pathogens to sun.

symptoms_text: Small circular dark brown spots on upper surface of leaves surrounded by yellow halo. Spots are 1-10mm in diameter. In severe infection, spots coalesce and leaves dry up and fall off. Defoliation leads to reduced pod filling.
symptoms_visual: Round to irregular dark brown spots with distinct yellow halo on leaf surface. Spots visible on both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Heavy defoliation in advanced stages leaving bare branches. Brown spots may also appear on petioles and stems.
favorable_conditions: High humidity above 80%, temperature 20-25°C, frequent rainfall, close spacing, excessive nitrogen
critical_crop_stage: 45-50 days after sowing to pod maturity
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like GAUG-10, GG-20. Practice 2-3 year crop rotation. Remove crop residues. Maintain proper plant spacing. Avoid excessive nitrogen.
chemical_management: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L at disease onset. Follow with Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L after 15 days. Alternate sprays at 15-day intervals. Chlorothalonil 75% WP at 2g/L is also effective.
biological_management: Apply Trichoderma harzianum as soil treatment. Neem oil spray at 5ml/L as preventive measure.
prevention: Early sowing to avoid peak disease period. Use disease-free seed. Remove volunteer groundnut plants. Deep plowing after harvest.
source: DGR Junagadh / JAU Package of Practices

symptoms_text: Small orange-brown to reddish-brown pustules on lower surface of leaves. Pustules are circular, raised, and powdery. Corresponding yellowish spots on upper leaf surface. Severe infection causes defoliation and reduced pod yield.
symptoms_visual: Orange-brown powdery pustules (uredinia) primarily on lower leaf surface. Upper leaf surface shows chlorotic yellow spots opposite to pustules. Leaves turn brown and dry in severe cases. Rust-colored powder visible when leaf is tapped.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 20-28°C, high humidity, heavy dew, late planted crops, cloudy weather
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties. Early sowing (first fortnight of June). Avoid late planting. Remove volunteer plants. Practice crop rotation.
chemical_management: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L water at disease onset. Hexaconazole 5% EC at 2ml/L or Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L are very effective. Two to three sprays at 15-day intervals.
biological_management: Limited biological options. Neem oil as supplementary measure.
prevention: Timely sowing. Avoid excess nitrogen. Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest. Use certified seed.

symptoms_text: Rotting at the collar region (base of stem at soil level). White fungal growth visible at base of plant. Plant wilts suddenly and dies. Brown to black discoloration at collar region. In Sclerotium infection, white mustard seed-like sclerotia visible on soil surface near plant base.
symptoms_visual: White cottony fungal growth at soil line around stem base. Plant suddenly wilts with leaves drooping. Dark brown to black lesion girdling the stem at ground level. Small round brown sclerotia (like mustard seeds) scattered on soil near infected plants.
favorable_conditions: High soil moisture, poor drainage, soil temperature 25-30°C, acidic soils, heavy rains followed by dry spells
cultural_management: Seed treatment before sowing is essential. Use well-drained fields. Avoid waterlogging. Practice crop rotation with cereals. Deep summer plowing. Apply lime at 500kg/ha in acidic soils.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Thiram 75% WP at 3g/kg seed or Carbendazim at 2g/kg. Soil drenching with Carbendazim 1g/L around plant base. Apply Trichoderma enriched FYM.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride at 4g/kg seed. Apply Trichoderma enriched FYM at 2.5kg/ha in soil at sowing. Pseudomonas fluorescens seed treatment.
prevention: Ensure good drainage. Treat seed before sowing. Avoid sowing in waterlogged fields. Remove previous crop debris.

symptoms_text: Yellow to orange-yellow pustules arranged in stripes along leaf veins. Pustules appear on upper leaf surface in linear rows. Leaves turn yellow and dry from tips. Severe infection reduces grain filling significantly.
symptoms_visual: Bright yellow-orange powdery pustules arranged in long linear stripes parallel to leaf veins. This stripe pattern distinguishes it from other rusts. Leaves appear striped yellow. In severe cases entire leaf turns yellow-brown.
favorable_conditions: Cool temperature 10-15°C with high humidity, cloudy weather, heavy dew, north India plains in January-February
cultural_management: Grow resistant varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, GW-322. Timely sowing (November). Avoid late sowing. Balanced fertilization, avoid excess nitrogen. Remove volunteer wheat and grassy weeds.
chemical_management: Spray Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L at first appearance. Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1ml/L. Triadimefon 25% WP at 1g/L. One to two sprays at 15-day intervals are sufficient.
biological_management: Limited biological options for rusts. Focus on resistant varieties.
prevention: Use resistant varieties as primary defense. Timely sowing. Destroy volunteer wheat plants. Monitor fields regularly after January.

symptoms_text: White to grayish powdery growth on upper leaf surface, leaf sheaths, and stems. Starts as small white patches that spread to cover entire leaf. Affected leaves turn yellow then brown. Black dots (cleistothecia) may appear in powdery growth in advanced stages.
symptoms_visual: White-gray powder-like coating on leaf surfaces, easily wiped off with finger. Starts as small circular white patches that expand and merge. Underlying leaf tissue turns yellow then brown. Entire plant may appear dusted with white powder in severe cases.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 15-22°C, high humidity 70-80%, cloudy weather, dense crop canopy, excessive nitrogen fertilization
cultural_management: Grow resistant varieties. Avoid excess nitrogen. Maintain proper spacing for air circulation. Remove infected crop debris after harvest.
chemical_management: Spray Sulfur 80% WP at 3g/L or Karathane (Dinocap) 48% EC at 1ml/L. Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L. Triadimefon 25% WP at 1g/L. Spray at first sign of disease.
biological_management: Foliar spray of Ampelomyces quisqualis based bio-fungicide where available.
prevention: Use resistant varieties. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Ensure proper plant spacing. Early sowing.

symptoms_text: Dark brown to black necrotic spots on leaves and stems. Spots have concentric rings giving target-board appearance. Stems show dark brown elongated lesions. Infected umbels turn brown and dry up. Seeds become shriveled and discolored. Can cause 80-100% yield loss in severe epidemics.
symptoms_visual: Dark brown spots with concentric ring pattern (target-like) on leaves. Stem lesions are elongated and dark. Flower umbels turn brown-black and dry up prematurely. Entire plant may appear scorched/burnt in severe cases.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 20-25°C, relative humidity above 70%, cloudy weather, frequent rainfall or heavy dew, dense canopy
critical_crop_stage: Flowering to seed formation (60-90 days after sowing)
cultural_management: Use resistant/tolerant varieties like GC-4, RZ-19. Wide spacing (30x15 cm). Avoid excessive irrigation during flowering. Remove infected plant debris. Practice 3-year crop rotation.
chemical_management: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L as preventive at 45 days. Iprodione 50% WP at 2g/L at disease onset. Difenconazole 25% EC at 0.5ml/L. Three sprays at 15-day intervals from flowering.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Trichoderma harzianum at 4g/kg. Pseudomonas fluorescens based formulation as soil and seed treatment.
prevention: Timely sowing (November first week). Avoid late sowing. Use treated seed. Proper spacing. Avoid excess nitrogen and irrigation during flowering.

symptoms_text: Sudden wilting of plants. Lower leaves turn yellow first then entire plant wilts. Plants dry up while still standing. Vascular tissue shows brown discoloration when stem is split. Plants can be easily pulled out due to root rotting. Disease appears in patches in the field.
symptoms_visual: Plants wilt suddenly, leaves droop and turn pale yellow then brown. Plants dry up in standing position. Patches of dead plants visible in field. Root system is poorly developed and shows brown discoloration internally.
favorable_conditions: Soil temperature 20-25°C, waterlogged conditions, acidic soils, continuous cumin cultivation, nematode infestation
critical_crop_stage: Seedling to flowering (30-75 days after sowing)
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like GC-4. Practice 3-4 year crop rotation with wheat, mustard, or barley. Avoid waterlogging. Deep summer plowing. Apply FYM liberally. Raise soil pH with lime if acidic.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Carbendazim 50% WP at 2g/kg + Thiram at 2g/kg. Soil drenching with Carbendazim 1g/L at disease onset. Apply Trichoderma enriched FYM at sowing.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride at 4g/kg. Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens in soil at 2.5kg/ha. Use neem cake at 150kg/ha for nematode suppression.
prevention: Long crop rotation. Avoid fields with wilt history. Use treated certified seed. Ensure good drainage. Apply organic matter.

symptoms_text: Drooping and yellowing of lower leaves. Progressive wilting from bottom to top. Plants dry up with leaves still attached. Dark brown to black discoloration of vascular bundles visible when stem is cut. Roots may show rotting symptoms.
symptoms_visual: Lower leaves turn yellow and droop. Wilting progresses upward. Stem cross-section shows dark brown-black discolored vascular tissue in ring pattern. Dead plant remains standing with dried leaves hanging.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 25-30°C, poorly drained soils, continuous castor cropping, acidic soils, nematode-infested soils
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like GCH-7, 48-1. Practice 3-4 year crop rotation with cereals. Apply lime in acidic soils. Use balanced fertilization. Remove infected plants. Deep summer plowing.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Carbendazim at 2g/kg + Thiram at 2g/kg. Soil drenching with Carbendazim 1g/L. Apply neem cake at 150kg/ha to suppress nematodes.
biological_management: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride 4g/kg. Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens at 2.5kg/ha. Trichoderma enriched FYM at sowing time.
prevention: Crop rotation is most important. Avoid continuous castor. Manage soil nematodes. Use wilt-resistant varieties only.

symptoms_text: Grey moldy growth on spikes and capsules. Capsules turn brown-grey and rot. Infected spikes show grey fuzzy fungal growth covering entire spike. Seeds become shriveled and discolored. Can spread rapidly in wet weather causing complete spike rot.
symptoms_visual: Grey fuzzy mold covering castor spikes and capsules. Infected spikes droop and turn greyish-brown. Entire spike may be covered in grey woolly fungal mass. Capsules become soft and rotten.
favorable_conditions: High humidity above 85%, temperature 20-25°C, continuous rain during flowering, dense planting, poor air circulation
cultural_management: Wide spacing for air circulation. Remove and destroy infected spikes. Avoid dense planting. Timely harvest of mature spikes. Remove volunteer plants.
chemical_management: Spray Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L at flowering onset. Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L. Two to three sprays at 15-day intervals during wet weather. Iprodione 50% WP at 2g/L.
biological_management: Trichoderma harzianum based spray formulations. Maintain field hygiene.
prevention: Wide spacing (90x60 cm minimum). Monitor weather during flowering. Spray preventively if continuous rain predicted. Timely harvest.

symptoms_text: Chlorotic streaks on leaves running parallel to veins. White powdery fungal growth on lower leaf surface in early morning. Earhead (spike) transforms into leafy green structure instead of grain - this is the characteristic green ear symptom. Stunted plants with reduced tillering.
symptoms_visual: Green leafy growth replacing the normal grain spike (earhead) - spike transforms into mass of small green leafy structures. Leaves show yellow-green streaks parallel to veins. White cottony growth on underside of leaves visible in early morning dew.
favorable_conditions: High humidity, temperature 20-30°C, waterlogged conditions, continuous bajra cropping, infected seed
cultural_management: Use resistant hybrids like HHB-67 Improved, GHB-558. Rogue out infected plants before flowering. Avoid continuous bajra. Practice crop rotation with legumes. Avoid waterlogging.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Metalaxyl 35% SD at 6g/kg seed is highly effective. Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) at 2g/L if disease appears. Downy mildew requires systemic fungicides.
biological_management: Limited biocontrol options. Seed treatment with Trichoderma as supplementary measure.
prevention: Use treated certified seed. Metalaxyl seed treatment is the most effective prevention. Avoid saving seed from infected fields. Remove and destroy infected plants before they shed spores.

symptoms_text: Honey dew (sticky sweet liquid) drips from infected florets during flowering. Later, hard dark brown to black elongated sclerotia (ergot bodies) replace normal grains in the earhead. Sclerotia are larger than normal seeds and protrude from the spike.
symptoms_visual: Sticky honey-like droplets dripping from earhead florets. Dark brown to purple-black hard structures (sclerotia) replacing grains in spike. Sclerotia are elongated, curved, 1-2 cm long, protruding between normal grains. Ants may be attracted to honeydew.
favorable_conditions: High humidity during flowering, temperature 20-30°C, cloudy weather, rain during flowering, open-pollinated varieties
cultural_management: Use resistant hybrids. Remove infected earheads before sclerotia mature and fall to soil. Deep summer plowing to bury sclerotia. Avoid fields with ergot history for 2-3 years.
chemical_management: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L or Copper oxychloride at 3g/L at 50% flowering. Ziram 80% WP at 2g/L. Spray in evening when flowers are open.
biological_management: Limited options. Good field sanitation is primary control.
prevention: Use hybrid varieties (more resistant than composites). Synchronize flowering by uniform sowing. Remove ergot sclerotia from seed lots by salt water flotation (9% NaCl solution).

causal_organism: Mungbean yellow mosaic virus (transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci)
symptoms_text: Bright yellow and green patches on leaves creating mosaic pattern. Leaves show irregular yellow spots alternating with normal green areas. Severe infection causes entire leaf to turn yellow. Plants stunted with fewer pods. Pods may be small and distorted with fewer seeds.
symptoms_visual: Irregular bright yellow patches interspersed with normal green on leaves creating mosaic/mottled pattern. Yellowing increases with disease progression until entire leaves turn yellow. Plants appear stunted and pale compared to healthy plants.
favorable_conditions: High whitefly population, temperature 25-35°C, dry weather favoring whitefly, late sowing, kharif season
critical_crop_stage: Vegetative to flowering (20-45 days after sowing)
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like Meha (IPM 99-125), SML-668, Virat. Early sowing (first fortnight of July). Remove infected plants. Use yellow sticky traps. Avoid late sowing.
chemical_management: Control whitefly vector: Seed treatment with Imidacloprid 70% WS at 5g/kg seed. Foliar spray of Imidacloprid 17.8 SL at 0.5ml/L or Thiamethoxam 25 WG at 0.5g/L at 15 and 30 DAS.
biological_management: Neem oil 5ml/L spray as whitefly repellent. Release Encarsia formosa for biological whitefly control. Yellow sticky traps at 12/acre.
prevention: Grow resistant varieties. Early sowing. Seed treatment with systemic insecticide. Monitor whitefly population from emergence. Remove weed hosts of whitefly.

symptoms_text: White to greyish powdery growth on both surfaces of leaves, stems, and pods. Starts as small white patches that spread rapidly. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and fall off. Pods remain small with poor seed filling.
symptoms_visual: White-grey talcum powder-like coating covering leaf surfaces. Both sides of leaves affected. White powdery growth also visible on stems and pods. Leaves beneath the powder turn yellow and may drop off.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 20-25°C, relative humidity 60-70%, dry weather with cool nights, late sown crop
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties. Early sowing. Avoid dense planting. Remove infected plant debris. Practice crop rotation.
chemical_management: Spray Sulfur 80% WP at 3g/L or Karathane (Dinocap) 48% EC at 1ml/L. Hexaconazole 5% EC at 2ml/L. Two sprays at 10-12 day intervals.
biological_management: Neem oil spray 5ml/L as supplementary measure.
prevention: Use tolerant varieties. Timely sowing. Balanced fertilization. Proper spacing.

symptoms_text: Water-soaked dark lesions on stems near base. Rapid wilting and death of plants. Black discoloration spreading from base upward. White fungal growth may appear on lesions in humid conditions. Plants collapse suddenly. Affects plants in patches, especially in waterlogged areas.
symptoms_visual: Dark brown to black water-soaked lesions at stem base. Plant wilts rapidly and collapses. White fluffy growth may be seen on affected stems in early morning. Patches of dead plants in low-lying waterlogged areas of field.
favorable_conditions: Heavy rainfall, waterlogged conditions, poor drainage, temperature 25-30°C, dense planting
cultural_management: Ensure good drainage. Raise beds in heavy rainfall areas. Avoid waterlogging. Practice crop rotation. Remove infected plants immediately.
chemical_management: Seed treatment with Metalaxyl at 2g/kg. Spray Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (Ridomil Gold) at 2g/L. Soil drenching with Copper oxychloride 3g/L in affected patches.
biological_management: Trichoderma viride seed treatment at 4g/kg. Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens in soil.
prevention: Good drainage is most critical. Raised bed sowing. Treat seeds. Avoid low-lying waterlogged fields. Crop rotation.

symptoms_text: Circular to irregular brown spots on leaves with lighter center and dark brown margin. Spots are 2-8mm diameter. Multiple spots coalesce causing leaf blight. Severe defoliation in advanced stages. Spots may also appear on stems and capsules.
symptoms_visual: Brown circular spots with concentric rings and dark margins on leaves. Center of spots may appear grey-white. Multiple spots merge causing large necrotic patches. Lower leaves affected first, progressing upward.
favorable_conditions: High humidity, temperature 25-30°C, frequent rain, dense canopy, kharif season
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties. Proper spacing. Remove infected plant debris. Practice crop rotation. Avoid excess nitrogen.
chemical_management: Spray Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L at disease onset. Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L. Two to three sprays at 15-day intervals.
biological_management: Neem oil spray 5ml/L. Trichoderma based formulations.
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Timely sowing. Proper spacing. Balanced fertilization.

symptoms_text: Diamond-shaped or elliptical spots on leaves with grey center and dark brown margin. Spots are 1-2 cm long with pointed ends. Node blast causes blackening of stem nodes which break easily. Neck blast causes rotting at panicle base turning it white (empty panicle). Finger-shaped lesions on leaves.
symptoms_visual: Diamond/eye-shaped spots on leaves with grey-white center and dark brown-reddish border with pointed ends. Lesions may coalesce to kill entire leaf. Infected panicle neck turns brown-black causing panicle to break or hang. White empty panicles indicate neck blast.
favorable_conditions: Temperature 20-28°C, high humidity above 90%, frequent rain, excess nitrogen, upland or rain-fed conditions, susceptible varieties
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties like Gurjari, GR-11. Balanced nitrogen (avoid excess). Split nitrogen application. Maintain thin water layer. Remove weed hosts. Practice crop rotation.
chemical_management: Spray Tricyclazole 75% WP at 0.6g/L as preventive. Isoprothiolane 40% EC at 1.5ml/L. Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L. Spray at tillering and before panicle emergence.
biological_management: Pseudomonas fluorescens seed treatment and foliar spray. Trichoderma viride application in soil.
prevention: Use resistant varieties. Balanced fertilization - do not exceed 120kg N/ha. Silicon application strengthens cell walls. Remove stubble after harvest.

symptoms_text: Water-soaked lesions starting from leaf tips and margins, spreading downward. Lesions turn yellow then white-grey as they dry. Leaves dry from tip creating straw-colored dead tissue. Bacterial ooze (milky droplets) visible on lesions in early morning. Kresek phase causes wilting of young plants.
symptoms_visual: Yellowing starting from leaf tips and margins, progressing into long wavy lesions. Affected leaf portion turns straw-white/grey. Clear wavy boundary between dead (white) and living (green) tissue. Milky beads of bacterial ooze visible on fresh lesions in morning.
favorable_conditions: High temperature 25-34°C, high humidity, strong winds with rain, excess nitrogen, wounds from storms or insects, flooded conditions
cultural_management: Use resistant varieties. Balanced nitrogen fertilization. Drain excess water. Avoid clipping of seedling tips during transplanting. Remove weed hosts. Avoid working in wet fields.
chemical_management: Spray Streptocycline 100ppm + Copper oxychloride 0.25% at disease onset. Repeat at 10-day intervals. Avoid copper-based fungicides near harvest.
biological_management: Pseudomonas fluorescens seed treatment and foliar spray at 5g/L. Maintain field hygiene.
prevention: Use resistant varieties. Balanced NPK fertilization. Avoid excess nitrogen. Ensure proper drainage. Do not transplant in fields with known BLB history without resistant variety.
